pattern: Sorting both the edges and the queries by their weight limit turns every query into a snapshot of a slowly-growing union-find, so each query is answered by only ever adding edges, never removing them.
hints: If you answer queries from the smallest limit to the largest, the set of edges usable so far only ever grows, so you never need to rebuild a graph from scratch per query.
Sort the edges by distance and the queries by limit; then for each query in that order, union in every edge whose distance is still below the current limit before checking connectivity.

Use a union-find (disjoint set union) structure: two nodes have a path within the limit exactly when they end up in the same component once all edges below that limit have been unioned in; remember to answer each query in its ORIGINAL position, not the sorted order.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one query blocked by a tight limit
two nodes, single edge, limit exactly equal to the distance (strict-less fails)
two nodes, single edge, limit one above the distance succeeds
duplicate parallel edges between the same pair with different distances
completely disconnected pair, no edge touches either endpoint
queries not sorted by limit and repeating the same pair
single isolated node has no edges at all

stress.description: moderate random graph with many queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 400))
    m = min(size * 2, 800)
    edges = []
    for _ in range(m):
        u = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        v = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        while v == u:
            v = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        d = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        edges.append([u, v, d])
    q = min(size * 2, 400)
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q):
        p = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        r = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        while r == p:
            r = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        limit = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        queries.append([p, r, limit])
    return [n, edges, queries]
